url: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dT7j8YC5Rf4
title: The Macro Minute: How is the US consumer faring amid the latest adverse policy shock?

Happy Thursday out there, team 42. It's your skipper here, Darius Dale, to present our macro minute for Thursday, May 28th, 2026. As always, we'll start with the executive summary from today's lead-off morning note, so let's dive right in. Today's key macro question is, how is the US consumer faring amid the latest adverse policy shock? Our answer is splendidly, as perfectly presaged by a resilient US economy and paradigm C, aka run-it-hot themes. The lowest savings rate since June 2022, coupled with the lowest real income growth since March 2022, and above-trend real consumption growth, is compelling evidence of our West Village Montauk effect thesis. As predicted, our West Village Montauk effect thesis continues to power the resilient US economy through a sequence of adverse policy shocks throughout the Biden and Trump administrations. Decades of socialism for the rich and capitalism for the poor have made the resilient US economy seemingly and impervious to adverse policy shocks. Elsewhere, the April PC report supported the consensus outlook for slowing inflation, and makes the war Fed less likely to tighten monetary policy over the medium term. And before we transition to our 42 macro dashboard, uh, for those who may be new uh, to our our themes and theses, what we mean by the West Village Montauk effect thesis, uh, the summary of that, it's you know, it's detailed, but the summary is that because we have such a high stock of savings on the US household sector balance sheet, roughly about $12 trillion, which is up from less than $4 trillion prior just prior to the pandemic, that high very elevated stock of savings is allowing the consumer sector in aggregate to reduce its monthly flow of savings in a way that increases its monthly consumption. You know, said differently, the consumer is spending more of its income into the economy, and as has been doing so uh, basically ever since uh, you know, kind of early uh, to mid-2021, which is you know, I think we all did think back in in the summer of 2022, uh, when we really noticed it in the data, and it's been persisting since then, and it's one of the reasons why the US economy has remained resilient throughout adverse policy shock adverse policy shock throughout the Biden and Trump administrations. So, wrapping up with a question from our community. This one's titled supply demand imbalance in the Treasury bond market. Says when Darius says there is a supply demand imbalance in the Treasury bond market, isn't that just another way of saying nominal GDP will pace expectations? Our understanding has always been that Treasury yields are function of expected nominal growth. If yields continue to rise and and bond prices fall, that must coincide with the stronger than expected nominal GDP. There's no way that banks will refuse to buy Treasury bonds in a world where yields are greater than nominal GDP. The arbitrage is just too good to pass up. So, phenomenal question here. You know, the the short answer is you know, there's more to our our supply demand imbalance the geopolitically driven supply demand imbalance in the Treasury bond market thesis. This is on the demand side of the equation with nominal GDP persistently being above trend, which is a dynamic that we accurately forecasted when we first authored our investing in a four turning regime presentation back in the summer of 2023. We've had persistently elevated nominal GDP growth, which reduces the demand for these securities certainly relative to other capital assets. And so, that's one component of it. A second component of it is that you have geopolitical factors that are also reducing the demand for the Treasury market, which is essentially supported by 33% about a third of the Treasury market is owned by a foreign investors. And so, you think about it in Europe, they're remilitarizing. In Japan, they're pursuing reflation as policy that is kind of you know, causing issues in their bond market capital destruction in their bond market. And then in China, you have strategic decoupling, which is reducing China's demand for dollar base FX reserve accumulation. There's obviously other geopolitical factors to consider like the freezing of Russian Central Bank assets and the reduction in reserve demand that that caused uh the perspective of the US dollar. Uh there's other factors as well, but those are the primary large factors on the demand side of the equation. And one other factor I would highlight on the demand side of the equation is the fact that uh increasing share of the Treasury market is now in the hands of the private sector, which demands ex-ante units of return for the risk that we take in our portfolios as opposed to the central banks, uh which, you know, buy to implement monetary policy, foreign central banks which buy to implement the reserve uh FX reserve programs. And uh commercial banks which buy to implement regulatory policy. You know, the though the share of those price, you know, insensitive buyers has gone down, and the share of the price sensitive buyers has gone up. So, these are all factors on the demand side of the equation that are contributing to the geopolitically driven supply-demand imbalance in the Treasury bond market, which has been our core research view since we authored this theme back in the summer of 2023. Uh and so, on the supply side, obviously, I don't, you know, you can go [clears throat] to Yahoo Finance and and figure out that the US has a sovereign debt uh uh a supply problem from the perspective of the fiscal profligacy that we see in DC, and it's both parties. It's the the Democrats can't stop spending, Republicans can't stop cutting taxes, and the deficit continues to expand on a trending basis as a function of that. So, there's a lot of things that are kind of feeding into that. There's other factors as well, uh but probably less a little bit too esoteric uh for this program. So, we'll wrap it up there. Uh Dari Sattell here presenting our Macro Minute for Thursday, May 28th, 2026. Uh best of luck out there today. We'll catch back here on Monday. I'm going to be uh traveling to uh a conference uh this weekend, the Rebel Capitalist conference this weekend. So, we'll see you down in Orlando. Uh um you know, if you guys are out there with my friend George Gammon uh and his team. So, looking forward to that. So, should catch you back here on Monday. Cheers.
